title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Credit Prices Decline for the Third Consecutive Week in Anticipation of OAL's Decision
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(16th June â€“ 22ndÂ June)
  • A total of 128,243 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 56.82% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a fall of 42.22% WoW as 500,243 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $41.29/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 8.18% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 8.08% in price, reaching $48.36/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 7.63% WoW; on 24th June, the spot price stood at $45.50/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $48.63/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices witnessed a rise of 5.42% WoW. D4, D5 RIN prices increased by 20.62% WoW, while D6 RIN prices went up by 17.35%.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Type 1 trade prices continued to decline last week, while spot prices increased. On June 16th June, the Senate Finance Committee released its draft of the â€œBig, Beautiful Billâ€, under which there were some amendments under the 45Z tax credit framework. It reduces the credit to 20 percent for transportation fuels made using foreign feedstocks, prohibits negative emissions ratesâ€”except for fuels derived from animal manureâ€”and excludes indirect land use change emissions from lifecycle assessments, which could benefit certain biofuels. The credit is extended through 2031, adds rules to prevent double crediting and removes the special higher rate for sustainable aviation fuel after 2025. It also bars foreign-influenced or state-owned entities from claiming 45Z credits, reinforcing national control over clean fuel incentives.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

The average Type 1 prices recorded an 8.18% WoW fall, decreasing from $44.97 to $41.29 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 8.08% WoW, from $52.61 to $48.36. Type 1 trades saw 128,243 credits exchanged, reflecting a fall of 56.82% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a fall of 42.22%, reaching 500,243 credits. [caption id="attachment_599323" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 7.63% WoW, reaching $44.88/MT. On 24th June, the spot price was $45.50/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $48.63/MT as of 24th June. [caption id="attachment_599324" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

As per the data reported by CARB last week, 40 trades were reported in the market, with 5 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $75.00 for one Type 2 transaction at a volume of 5,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $40.00 per credit of Type 1 reported for two transactions at a volume of 14,394 and 25,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 35,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for a price of $54.07 and $54.29, and the smallest volume transfers of 2,500 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for a price of $42.50. [caption id="attachment_599325" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

The average spot price in June for LCFS Type 1 credits stood at $44.95 , lower than June 2024, through the week of June 16th, and the total number of trading volumes transacted for the same period stood at 2.85 MMT. [caption id="attachment_599326" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] Â CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 16th June â€“ 22nd June, total VPT recorded a 14.55% decline WoW, with a fall in all type 1 and non-zero credit prices for the sixth consecutive week. [caption id="attachment_599327" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] Â Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices witnessed a rise of 5.42% WoW. D4, D5 RIN prices increased by 20.62% WoW, while D6 RIN prices went up by 17.35%. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel Â 

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week
  • Senate's Reconcilliation Bill: Proposed Amendments to 45Z Credit Tighten Eligibility and Emission Rules: The Senate Finance Committee on June 16 released its draft of the â€œbig, beautiful bill.â€ The reconciliation bill proposes several changes to the 45Z tax credit framework. It prohibits the use of negative emission rates for calculating credit values and excludes emissions from indirect land use changes in lifecycle assessments.
  • US Supreme Court Sets Test for Which Courts Can Hear EPA Cases: The US Supreme Court established rules of the road on Wednesday to determine when lawsuits challenging actions by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency related to air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions should be heard by regional appeals courts or an appeals court in Washington that often hears regulatory cases.TheÂ 7-2 ruling, opens new tabheld that the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit, and not the New Orleans-based 5th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals, is the proper court to hear aÂ lawsuitÂ by small oil refiners challenging the EPA's denial of waivers exempting them from national biofuel mandates.
